title: Te Kōrero o te Awa: The Living Story of Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Te Kōrero Ora o te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a vital waterway in our rohe that holds ancestral stories of Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that traditionally grew along its banks, and its waters connect to local pūrākau about navigation, sustenance, and environmental guardianship.
learningObjectives: Identify and use 10+ Māori kupu related to waterways (awa, puna, kōawa) and environmental stewardship (kaitiakitanga, tiaki, taonga) in spoken and written contexts
Recount the pūrākau of Mangakōtukutuku Stream using appropriate sentence structures and past tense markers (i...ai)
Create a simple pepeha that incorporates the Mangakōtukutuku Stream as a significant local landmark
Express personal connections to local waterways using basic emotional and descriptive vocabulary (pai, ataahua, tino whakahirahira)

activities.title: He Hīkoi Māori: Sensory Exploration of Te Awa
activities.description: Guided walk along Mangakōtukutuku Stream where students use all senses to connect with the environment. They will listen for waiata of manu, feel different textures of leaves/water, and smell the native plants. Throughout, they'll learn and use specific kupu for what they experience (e.g., 'Kei te rongo ahau i te hau' - I feel the wind).

activities.title: Te Pūrākau o Mangakōtukutuku: Story Circle
activities.description: Seated near the awa, a local kaumatua shares the traditional story of how the stream was named and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students then work in small groups to retell key parts of the story using sequenced sentence cards with Māori temporal markers (i mua, i muri, nā reira).

activities.title: He Pepeha mō Te Awa: Personal Connection Crafting
activities.description: Using a template, students create their own pepeha that includes the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They learn to say: 'Ko Mangakōtukutuku te awa' and add a sentence about their relationship to it (e.g., 'Kei te noho ahau ki taha o te awa' - I live beside the stream).

activities.title: Ngā Kupu o Te Taiao: Vocabulary Treasure Hunt
activities.description: Around the school grounds (which border the stream), students find hidden cards with Māori words and matching images related to the awa ecosystem (kōtukutuku - fuchsia, inanga - whitebait, toitoi - plume grass). They work in pairs to match and practice pronunciation.

activities.title: He Waiata mō Te Awa: Song Creation
activities.description: Using a simple traditional melody, students collaboratively write a short waiata about caring for Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They incorporate learned vocabulary and phrases about protection (e.g., 'Me tiaki tātou i te awa' - We must care for the stream) and perform it for the class.

resources: Kaumatua Hōri Waititi (local Ngāti Kahungunu elder and storyteller)
Te Aka Māori Dictionary online or app
'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' book by local author Tāwhiri Waititi
Physical artifacts: traditional kōtukutuku bark dye, preserved inanga samples from stream

tasks: Record a 30-second audio clip describing one feature of Mangakōtukutuku Stream using at least 5 new vocabulary words
Create a simple visual poster with labeled drawings of 3 stream elements with Māori captions
Perform your personalized pepeha including the awa to a partner with correct pronunciation

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori to communicate information about familiar activities and events
Te Reo Māori: Language - Level 4: Recognize and use vocabulary and formulaic expressions related to familiar contexts
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organization - Level 4: Understand how people participate in communities and how communities influence people's lives
Science: Living World - Level 4: Explore how different groups in society view and use local environments
